mnemonics: MULTITAPE: 'M's in TMs' - 'Many Tapes' Need Extra Passes At Turnaround - O(n²) time is what we earn!
NDTM to DTM: BFS - 'Broadly Find Solution' - exploring all branches systematically
UTM: Universal Takes encoded Machine + input - stores program as data!
LBA: 'Limited By Allocation' - tape limited to input length

acronyms.usage: Extension with multiple tapes for convenience, O(n²) simulation on single-tape
Accepts if ANY path leads to accept; can be simulated by DTM with exponential overhead
Can simulate any other TM; foundation of stored-program computers
TM with tape limited to input length; accepts context-sensitive languages
Algorithm used to simulate NDTM by DTM - explores all paths level by level

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a single tape with vertical lines (#) separating 'lanes' - each lane is a virtual tape. To simulate one step, scan right across all lanes to read all head positions, then scan left updating each lane.
Draw a tree where each node is a configuration, branches are possible transitions. DTM explores this tree level-by-level (BFS). Accepting state = found at any level = ACCEPT.
Two-part input: LEFT = encoding of TM M (states, transitions), RIGHT = encoding of input w. The UTM reads M's rules and applies them to w - 'TM interpreting TM'.
Box the input with [ and ] markers. The head cannot go outside these markers. The entire computation happens within this bounded region - exactly n cells for input of length n.

analogies.analogy: Like having multiple notebooks open simultaneously vs. one notebook where you constantly flip between sections. Both can solve the same problems, but multiple is more convenient.
Like exploring a maze by trying ALL paths simultaneously (NDTM) vs. systematically marking and trying each path one by one (DTM). Both find exits if they exist, but the latter takes more time.
Like a programmable calculator (UTM) vs. a calculator with only addition (basic TM). The programmable one can perform ANY calculation by following different instruction sets.
Like a computer with limited memory (only n bytes for input of size n) vs. a computer with unlimited memory. Both can run the same programs, but LBA version must work within fixed bounds.
